title: Architecture
description: The blueprint that defines how a model's layers, modules, and data flow connect during inference.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: An architecture specifies layer types, connectivity, and the order of computation. Transformers, U-Net diffusion stacks, and mixture-of-experts layouts are architectures. Many model checkpoints can instantiate the same architecture at different scales.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Knowing the architecture tells you which modules to expect, how memory scales with sequence length, and which family pages in this atlas apply before you open a specific checkpoint. Ask "what repeats in the stack?" to spot the architecture; ask "which checkpoint am I running?" to spot the model.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: A decoder-only transformer architecture stacks token embeddings, repeated attention-plus-FFN blocks, and a final language-model head. GPT-2, Llama, and many chat models share that shape even when widths and depths differ.

whereItAppears.title: Where It Appears
whereItAppears.body: Architecture shows up in model cards, scaling-law papers, and module pages that explain attention variants. Related links below connect to major model families and structural concepts in this atlas.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: Architecture is broader than a single module: attention is a module inside many architectures. It is also narrower than "model": you can train many models on one architecture. A model family name often implies an architecture but may bundle training data and tooling too.

openingSummary: Architecture is the structural recipe—how tensors move through repeated modules—not any one trained set of weights, which people often conflate with specific checkpoints or product families.
